hooks.prompt: Check $ARGUMENTS for: rm -rf (/ ~ .), sudo rm, curl/wget piped to sh, git push --force, git reset --hard, DROP DATABASE/TABLE, chmod -R 777, production deploys (vercel --prod, npm publish, fly deploy, heroku push, aws deploy, gcloud deploy). If dangerous: {"ok": false, "reason": "Blocked: <pattern>"}. If prod deploy: {"ok": true, "reason": "Confirm with user."}. Otherwise: {"ok": true}

Check $ARGUMENTS file path. If sensitive (/etc/, /usr/, ~/.ssh/, ~/.aws/, ~/.gnupg/, ~/.npmrc, ~/.netrc, ~/.gitconfig): {"ok": false, "reason": "Write to sensitive path blocked."}. Otherwise: {"ok": true}

Check $ARGUMENTS file path extension. If web UI file (.tsx/.jsx/.vue/.svelte/.css/.scss/.html): note that IBR scan can return live CSS, handler, and a11y data for this file's output. If native UI file (.swift): note native_scan is available. Otherwise: do nothing. One sentence, informational not prescriptive.
If UI files (.tsx/.jsx/.vue/.svelte/.swift) were edited this session but IBR scan/native_scan was not used, mention scan data is available to confirm the implementation. One sentence. Do not block.

IBR is your design implementation partner — reads live UI and returns structured data (computed CSS, handlers, accessibility, page structure). Primary: `scan` (reads any URL). Also: `snapshot`/`compare` (track changes), `screenshot`/`references` (design references), `validate_tokens` (design spec checks). Native: `native_scan`, `scan_macos`. Web tools for .tsx/.jsx/.vue/.html, native for .swift.
